name: Amun
description: Amun, whose name signifies "the Hidden One," is a prominent Egyptian deity whose origins trace back to Thebes during the Old Kingdom. Initially a local god of the air and fertility, Amun's significance grew exponentially during the Middle Kingdom, eventually becoming the patron deity of Thebes. His association with the sun god Ra led to the syncretic deity Amun-Ra, representing the king of the gods, embodying both the hidden, mysterious aspects of creation and the visible power of the sun. Amun-Ra's cult became incredibly influential, with vast temples and priesthoods dedicated to his worship. He was often depicted as a ram-headed man or a man wearing a headdress with two tall plumes, symbolizing his power and authority. Amun's role evolved to encompass not only kingship and creation but also justice and protection, solidifying his place as a central figure in the Egyptian pantheon for centuries.
subtitle: King of the Gods
titles: Lord of the Thrones of the Two Lands

keyMyths.title: Amun's Rise to Prominence
keyMyths.description: Originally a local Theban deity, Amun's importance increased dramatically during the Middle Kingdom. The Theban rulers elevated Amun to the status of a national god, associating him with kingship and military success. This rise in power was facilitated by Thebes becoming the capital of Egypt, allowing Amun's cult to spread and influence the entire country. His assimilation with Ra further cemented his position as the supreme deity, symbolizing the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt under a single divine authority.
keyMyths.source: Assmann, Jan. *The Search for God in Ancient Egypt*.

keyMyths.title: Amun and the Divine Birth of Hatshepsut
keyMyths.description: One of the most famous myths surrounding Amun involves the divine birth of the female pharaoh Hatshepsut. According to the myth, Amun visited Queen Ahmose in the guise of her husband, Thutmose I, and impregnated her, thus ensuring Hatshepsut's legitimacy and divine right to rule. This story, prominently depicted in Hatshepsut's mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri, served to reinforce her authority and connection to the divine realm, despite the societal norms that favored male rulers.

keyMyths.title: Amun and the Oracle at Siwa
keyMyths.description: The Oracle of Amun at Siwa Oasis held significant political and religious sway, even beyond the borders of Egypt. Alexander the Great famously consulted the oracle, where he was allegedly recognized as the son of Amun. This event played a crucial role in legitimizing Alexander's rule in Egypt and further solidified the association between Amun and powerful rulers, demonstrating the enduring influence of the deity in the ancient world.

sources.author: Assmann, Jan
sources.work: The Search for God in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of the development of religious thought in ancient Egypt, including the rise of Amun.

sources.author: Allen, James P.
sources.work: Genesis in Egypt: The Philosophy of Ancient Egyptian Creation Accounts
sources.description: Examines the various creation myths in ancient Egypt, including Amun's role as a creator god.
sources.text: Examines the various creation myths in ancient Egypt, including Amun's role as a creator god.

sources.author: Teeter, Emily
